title: CSR: Confidentiality & Client Trust
description: A CSR course on privacy, discretion, record handling, lobby conversations, social media caution, and protecting trust in the veterinary-client-patient relationship.

modules.title: Module 1 — Trust at the Front Desk

blocks.prompt: A lobby client asks why another dog was seen first.

options.text: Explain the other dog’s condition.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Give a general answer about prioritizing medical need without revealing another patient’s details.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Complain about the schedule publicly.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: CSRs handle sensitive client details, medical histories, finances, euthanasia appointments, and medication requests. Confidentiality means sharing only with authorized people for appropriate patient-care or business reasons.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Protecting client and patient information from unauthorized sharing.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: You are leaving a voicemail about a sensitive case.

options.text: Describe the entire case loudly.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Follow clinic voicemail policy and avoid unnecessary detail.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Leave billing and medical details with an unknown person.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Privacy risks include loud phone conversations, visible screens, records left out, wrong email addresses, too much voicemail detail, and casual case discussion.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What should CSRs slow down for?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Records, emails, phone numbers, voicemails, and sensitive conversations.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

lessons.title: The Case Is Not Yours to Share

blocks.prompt: A sad case affected you and you want to post “no names.”

options.text: Post because no names are listed.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Do not post without clinic approval and proper permission.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Use initials and assume it is fine.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Even without names, details can identify a client or patient. Do not post client stories, patient cases, complaints, or clinic incidents unless handled through approved clinic processes with permission.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What is the safest social media rule?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Do not post clinic/client/patient information unless approved.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: No names does not always mean anonymous.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: A client is upset about finances in a full lobby.

options.text: Discuss their balance loudly.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Offer discreet help and follow policy without broadcasting details.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Tell other clients what is happening.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Sensitive conversations should be moved away from public spaces when possible. Use lower volume, private rooms, or written follow-up according to workflow.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Client dignity, privacy, and clinic trust.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.
